summary: Southeast BeltLine neighborhoods are buzzing with new developments this week, from Reynoldstown breaking ground on townhomes to Inman Park welcoming Mushi Ni's Asian-American comfort food. Meanwhile, the airport chaos from the government shutdown and new food options at Truist Park are keeping Atlanta talking.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown BeltLine Development Finally Breaks Ground After 5-Year Wait
topStory.summary: After sitting empty for nearly five years, that cleared corner at Chester and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown is finally seeing action. Atlanta developer Embry Development Company has started construction on 85 townhomes and condos, taking over from Toll Brothers who originally permitted the site. It's right by the BeltLine Eastside Trail, which explains why people have been so eager to see something happen here.
topStory.body:
You know that cleared lot on Chester Avenue where it meets Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown? The one that's been sitting empty since they tore down whatever was there back in 2021? Well, finally, finally there's some movement. Embry Development Company confirmed they've started groundwork on what they're calling the Camber Crossing project, and honestly, it's about time.

The 2-acre site is going to have 85 residential units, a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos. Embry bought it from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago and took over the existing permits rather than starting from scratch. Smart move, considering how long permitting takes these days. The location is golden, just a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail and a block north of Memorial Drive.

What's interesting is how this project represents the ongoing transformation of Reynoldstown. Five years ago, this was just another forgotten corner. Now it's prime real estate because of the BeltLine connection. Embry's managing partner says they're targeting people who want to live close to the trail but still have that urban convenience. Given how packed the BeltLine gets on weekends, I'd say they're betting on the right horse.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site showing groundwork beginning at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Housing Village About to Launch
stories.summary: The Cottages at Midway is getting ready for its soft launch next week, bringing 18 standalone houses around shared courtyards and greenspace. It's designed as more affordable ITP housing near downtown Decatur, where median home values have climbed over $700,000. The pocket neighborhood sits on 2.1 acres of formerly underused lots on Midway Road.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices pushing past $700,000, finding anything remotely affordable ITP has become a real challenge. That's where the Cottages at Midway comes in, and they're about to start their soft launch next week. This is exactly the kind of 'missing middle' housing we need more of in metro Atlanta.

The development sits on Midway Road in unincorporated Decatur, turning two underused lots totaling 2.1 acres into a village-style community of 18 standalone houses. What makes this different from typical subdivisions is the focus on shared spaces, courtyards, and greenspace that actually brings neighbors together. It's built around human connection rather than just maximizing square footage.

The timing couldn't be better. With downtown Decatur's charm driving up prices throughout the area, projects like this offer a middle ground between expensive single-family homes and cramped apartments. The developers are specifically targeting people who want to stay ITP but can't afford the typical asking prices. If this model works, we could see more pocket neighborhoods like this sprouting up in other close-in communities.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing layout of Cottages at Midway development with shared courtyards

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: The GW Parkside project (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is making real progress along the Southwest BeltLine corridor. Ackerman & Co. has 23 new townhomes planned at 600 Hopkins Street, with the first seven units nearing completion and coming to market this spring. It's rare to find for-sale townhomes along this stretch of the BeltLine.
stories.body:
The Southwest BeltLine corridor has been slower to develop than its eastside counterpart, but projects like GW Parkside are starting to change that equation. Ackerman & Co., the same folks behind Lee + White's food and office scene, started this townhome project a year ago and it's finally taking shape.

What you're seeing at 600 Hopkins Street is 23 new townhomes, with the first seven nearly topped out and hitting the market this spring. They've rebranded from the original 'Hopkins and White Townhomes' name to 'Gordon White Parkside,' or GW Parkside for short. The location puts you right on the BeltLine's Westside Trail, which connects you to everything from West End to the core of the BeltLine loop.

The interesting thing about this project is how rare for-sale townhomes are along this stretch. Most of what we've seen on the Westside Trail has been apartments and condos. These townhomes give people a chance to own something with a little more space and privacy while still being plugged into the BeltLine network. Plus, with Lee + White expanding its food and retail offerings nearby, the whole area is becoming more of a destination rather than just a pass-through.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. After years of pop-ups, they're now serving everything from soft shell crab bao to Peking chicken platters alongside creative cocktails like Thai tea martinis. The location puts them perfectly positioned for both neighborhood regulars and trail traffic.
stories.body:
Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez have been building their rep through pop-ups around town, but today they finally have their own brick and mortar spot. Mushi Ni opened this morning in Inman Park, and the timing couldn't be better with spring weather bringing more people to the Freedom Park Trail.

The menu is what they call Asian-American comfort food, which means dishes like soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, and their signature Peking chicken that feeds two people for $25. They're also doing snacks like umami fries and crab Rangoon puffs, plus a full cocktail program with drinks like tamarind whiskey sours and Thai tea martinis. Both chefs have serious credentials (Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York), so this isn't just fusion for fusion's sake.

The Elizabeth Street location is smart positioning. You're right in the heart of Inman Park but also catching foot traffic from people using the Freedom Park Trail. Plus, that stretch of Elizabeth has been quietly building up its restaurant scene over the past few years. With lunch and dinner service, they can catch both the work-from-home crowd and the dinner date scene. If the food lives up to their pop-up reputation, this could become a real neighborhood anchor.
stories.imageAlt: Interior view of Mushi Ni restaurant with dining tables and Asian-inspired decor

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens Near East Lake in April
stories.summary: The creator of NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food hall to the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining, opening mid-April. This eastside location adds to Montwaid's Ponce City Market and Krog Street Market empire.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid has become Atlanta's unofficial food hall king with Ponce City Market and Krog Street Market, and now he's expanding eastward. His latest project, Vela Park Food Works, is finishing construction at the new Vela Park rental community near East Lake Golf Club and should open in mid-April.

This one follows his proven formula of bringing together six different, chef-driven food concepts under one roof, along with craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. The location inside the nearly 12-acre Vela Park development puts it in an interesting spot, serving both the new residents and drawing food lovers from across the eastside. Think of it as the eastside's answer to his other successful markets.

What's smart about this expansion is how it fills a real gap in the eastside food scene. While we've got great individual restaurants scattered around neighborhoods like Candler Park, Kirkwood, and East Lake, there hasn't been a central food hall destination on this side of town. Plus, with East Lake continuing to develop and property values rising, the timing seems right for a higher-end food destination. If Montwaid's track record holds, this could become another must-visit spot for both locals and food tourists.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing multiple food stalls and dining areas

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to the BeltLine
stories.summary: The acclaimed pitmaster John Lewis has opened his latest outpost at Ansley Mall, complete with a spectacular upstairs smokehouse overlooking the new BeltLine pedestrian bridge. Six 22-foot smokers run 24/7 in what looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia, turning out some of the city's best brisket. Lewis helped weld the smokers himself from 1,000-gallon propane tanks.
stories.body:
John Lewis has been making waves in the barbecue world from Charleston to Austin, and now he's brought his smoke magic to Atlanta in a pretty spectacular way. The Lewis Barbecue location at Ansley Mall isn't your typical strip-mall joint. They've put the smokehouse upstairs in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine, and it's honestly unlike anything I've seen.

Six massive 22-foot smokers fill the space, running in staggered eight-hour shifts around the clock. Lewis actually helped weld these things himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, which explains why the brisket coming out is so consistently excellent. The whole operation runs like a factory, but the end result is some of the best barbecue you'll find in Atlanta. The loaded platter balances smoke and heat perfectly, with sides that actually complement rather than compete with the meat.

The location is perfect timing with the BeltLine connection. You can walk or bike over from Piedmont Park or Virginia-Highland and work up an appetite, then settle in for some serious barbecue. Plus, being at Ansley Mall near The Cook's Warehouse means you're already in food lover territory. Lewis has a reputation for elevating barbecue beyond typical expectations, and this Atlanta location delivers on that promise. The upstairs smokehouse alone is worth the visit, but the food will keep you coming back.
stories.imageAlt: Platter of barbecue from Lewis Barbecue showing brisket, ribs and sides

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Project Transforms West Howard Avenue
stories.summary: After nearly a decade in planning, Decatur's push to turn West Howard Avenue from a drag strip into a people-friendly Complete Street is almost done. The project includes a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle near East Lake MARTA station. It's a major win for anyone who's ever tried to bike or walk that corridor.
stories.body:
Anyone who's tried to bike or walk down West Howard Avenue knows it's been a nightmare for years. Cars fly down that stretch like it's their personal highway, making it dangerous for everyone else. But Decatur's Complete Streets project, which kicked off last August, is finally turning that around.

The roughly one-mile transformation runs from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) all the way to Paden Circle at the northern edge of East Lake MARTA station. What you're getting is a two-way cycle track with actual concrete barriers protecting cyclists from traffic, plus upgraded sidewalks and infrastructure throughout. The traffic calming measures should finally make drivers think twice about treating this like a speedway.

This project has been in the works for nearly a decade, which tells you how long the city has been trying to solve this problem. The timing makes sense with more people biking and walking, especially with connections to the MARTA station and downtown Decatur. It's part of a broader trend of cities recognizing that streets should serve everyone, not just cars. Once this is fully completed, it should be a game-changer for anyone commuting by bike or just trying to get around Decatur without a car.
stories.imageAlt: New bike lane with concrete barriers on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls While Fire Union Contract Stalls
stories.summary: Atlanta's Public Safety Committee unanimously passed two resolutions to limit ICE activities in the city, heading to full council vote April 20. Meanwhile, firefighters packed the meeting to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign their first-ever union contract that council ratified last April. The contrast highlights ongoing tensions between the mayor and council.
stories.body:
The Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee made some significant moves Monday, unanimously advancing two resolutions that would seriously limit ICE operations within city limits. One bars ICE detention facilities in Atlanta, while the other requires police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. Both head to the full council vote on April 20.

But the bigger drama came from dozens of firefighters who showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign their union contract. The council ratified Atlanta's first-ever firefighter union contract back in April 2025, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since. The firefighters made it clear they're not happy about working under an agreement their mayor won't even acknowledge.

This whole situation really captures the ongoing tension between Dickens and the council. While they're finding common ground on immigration policy (both resolutions got unanimous support), labor relations remain a major sticking point. The firefighters have a legitimate gripe here - if the council approved their contract, what's the mayor waiting for? It's the kind of political standoff that makes everyone look bad while the actual workers get caught in the middle.
stories.imageAlt: Firefighters gathered at Atlanta City Council meeting

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb County
stories.summary: A 7-year-old boy died overnight Wednesday after accidentally shooting himself at a home on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area. Family members told reporters the child was playing with a gun when it discharged. DeKalb County police are investigating what they're calling a 'non-intended discharge of a weapon.'
stories.body:
This is the kind of story that just breaks your heart. A 7-year-old boy died early Wednesday morning in what DeKalb County police are calling an accidental shooting at a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood, off Bouldercrest Road. Family members at the scene said the child was playing with a gun when it went off.

Police investigators confirmed off-camera that they're treating this as a 'non-intended discharge of a weapon,' which is their way of saying this was a tragic accident. The scene drew a large crowd of grieving family and friends to the neighborhood, and you could feel the devastation from everyone involved. The roadway was blocked off for several hours while investigators worked.

This incident highlights something we've talked about too many times in Atlanta and across the country: the absolute necessity of keeping firearms away from children. Whatever the circumstances that led to this child having access to a gun, it's a reminder that gun safety isn't just about training adults, it's about creating barriers that prevent these kinds of tragedies. A 7-year-old shouldn't have been able to get his hands on a firearm, period. Now a family is destroyed and a community is mourning a completely preventable loss.
stories.imageAlt: Police vehicles at scene of investigation on Loveless Drive

stories.headline: Climate Leaders Fight Urban Heat Islands in Underserved Communities
stories.summary: At Monday's Parks and Greenspace Conference, UrbanHeatATL and other groups highlighted how underserved Atlanta communities experience disproportionate heat islands despite the city's 'city in a forest' reputation. Extreme heat is the leading cause of weather-related deaths in the US, making this a serious public health issue for neighborhoods without adequate tree cover.
stories.body:
Atlanta calls itself the 'city in a forest' because of our dense urban tree canopy, but that nickname doesn't tell the whole story. At Monday's Parks and Greenspace Conference at the Atlanta Botanical Garden, climate justice leaders made it clear that underserved communities across metro Atlanta are experiencing dangerous heat islands that can literally be deadly.

UrbanHeatATL is encouraging residents in these affected neighborhoods to build coalitions and engage with property management companies to push for more tree coverage and cooling infrastructure. According to the EPA, extreme heat is actually the leading cause of weather-related deaths in the United States, which makes this more than just a comfort issue. We're talking about life and death, especially for elderly residents and people without air conditioning.

The conference brought together hundreds of attendees from across the Southeast to tackle these resilience challenges. What's encouraging is seeing community organizers take this issue head-on rather than waiting for city government to solve everything. When you look at satellite heat maps of Atlanta, the disparities are stark. Wealthier neighborhoods stay cooler because of tree cover, while working-class communities bake in the sun. UrbanHeatATL's approach of building grassroots pressure for change feels like the right strategy, because these communities know their needs better than anyone sitting in an air-conditioned office downtown.
stories.imageAlt: Community members gathered at Parks and Greenspace Conference discussing urban heat solutions
